items.mistake: Diagnosing a leak from one snapshot (totals, not deltas).
items.fix: Take two snapshots around a suspect operation; use `compare_to` (or `memray --leaks`).
items.why: A single 'top allocations' view shows fat — useful — but doesn't show *growth*; leaks need a delta.
items.example:
```python
before = tracemalloc.take_snapshot()
for _ in range(10): do_op()
after = tracemalloc.take_snapshot()
print(after.compare_to(before, 'lineno')[:5])
```

items.fix: Use a contiguous numpy array (`np.zeros((n, d), dtype='float32')`).
items.why: Python objects have ~28 bytes of per-object overhead each; a list of 1M floats can be 20-50× larger than the numpy equivalent.
items.example: 1M × 768-dim list ≈ several GB; numpy float32 ≈ 3 GB. Float16 halves it again.

items.fix: Always set a `maxsize` (often 1k-100k) and consider a TTL eviction.
items.why: `maxsize=None` keeps every unique key forever — a textbook leak when keys are user-derived.

items.mistake: Profiling memory without disabling caching layers / warm-ups.
items.fix: Run the suspect operation once to warm caches, then start profiling.
items.why: Cold-cache numbers include startup allocations and mislead about steady-state behaviour.
items.example: First call loads tokenizer + model — that's not the leak you're hunting.

items.fix: When tracemalloc shows a single big numpy line, audit dtype and reshape; FAISS supports mmap for big indices.
items.why: Python-side tracing can summarise massive C allocations as a single point — read those carefully.
items.example: FAISS index: `faiss.read_index(path, faiss.IO_FLAG_MMAP)` pages from disk on demand.
